steps.title: Identify what punctuation the sentence needs
steps.content: The sentence has a main noun phrase — “24.5-by-34.5 inch oil [painting Gun with Hand #1]” — followed by a nonrestrictive relative clause (“which was created in 1964”). A nonrestrictive clause must be set off with a comma before “which.” That comma belongs after the painting title, not inside it. No comma should appear between “oil painting” and the title itself, because the title is a direct appositive that identifies the painting.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — painting *Gun with Hand #1*: No comma after the title, so the nonrestrictive clause “which was created…” is not set off. Violates the rule that nonrestrictive clauses require a comma. ✗
  • Choice B — painting, *Gun with Hand #1*,: Inserts a comma between “painting” and its identifying title, incorrectly separating an appositive that is essential to naming the specific work. ✗
  • Choice C — painting *Gun with Hand #1*,: Keeps “painting” and its title together (no spurious comma between them) and places the required comma after the title to introduce the nonrestrictive clause. ✓
  • Choice D — painting, *Gun with Hand #1*: Inserts a comma between “painting” and its title and omits the comma after the title, producing two errors simultaneously. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
